Clonal Expansion & Dominance Analysis

TCR Repertoire Analysis — Notebook 03

Joshua Luthy

2026-03-15

Analysis Overview

This notebook examines clonal expansion patterns in the manufactured Product, identifying the most dominant clonotypes and characterizing the degree of oligoclonality. We assess whether specific clones dominate the Product and how the top clone fraction varies with clinical response.

tcr_data <- read_csv("../data/raw/synthetic_tcr_repertoires.csv")
top_clones <- read_csv("../data/processed/top_clonotypes.csv")
patient_meta <- read_csv("../data/raw/patient_metadata.csv")

Top Expanded Clonotypes

Below are the top 10 expanded clonotypes in the Product for patient PT-001 (CR). Note the extreme dominance of the top clone.

top_clones %>%
  filter(patient_id == "PT-001", sample_type == "Product") %>%
  head(10) %>%
  mutate(clone_fraction_pct = paste0(round(clone_fraction * 100, 2), "%")) %>%
  select(rank, cdr3_aa, v_gene, j_gene, clone_count, clone_fraction_pct) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Top 10 expanded clonotypes — PT-001 (CR)",
    format.args = list(big.mark = ",")
  )
Top 10 expanded clonotypes — PT-001 (CR)
rank cdr3_aa v_gene j_gene clone_count clone_fraction_pct
1 CDWVSYQFTKRRF TRBV7-9 TRBJ1-1 30,790 42.46%
2 CMGDVHRMPPGLMF TRBV29-1 TRBJ2-1 5,447 7.51%
3 CYSAQCWFMKLMYEF TRBV29-1 TRBJ2-1 1,988 2.74%
4 CLICPRQLPLMVNKLF TRBV10-3 TRBJ1-2 980 1.35%
5 CETHNLRSMQAPQSIQVF TRBV12-4 TRBJ1-6 565 0.78%
6 CVIYIPQNADGMSHIGF TRBV6-5 TRBJ2-5 368 0.51%
7 CINEITGPPDMNGIVF TRBV18 TRBJ1-5 239 0.33%
8 CWMWNDKGQWESRWEWIF TRBV15 TRBJ2-4 185 0.26%
9 CEWFQHPQDVWDRIAF TRBV30 TRBJ2-3 128 0.18%
10 CLGIVHPSSGGAHPVVF TRBV27 TRBJ1-1 107 0.15%

Clonal Dominance Patterns

We quantify clonal dominance by examining the fraction of total reads held by the top N clonotypes in each Product sample.

cumulative <- top_clones %>%
  filter(sample_type == "Product") %>%
  group_by(patient_id, clinical_response) %>%
  arrange(rank) %>%
  mutate(cum_fraction = cumsum(clone_fraction)) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(cumulative, aes(x = rank, y = cum_fraction,
                        color = clinical_response, group = patient_id)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(x = "Clonotype Rank", y = "Cumulative Read Fraction",
       color = "Response",
       title = "Cumulative Clonal Dominance in Product",
       subtitle = "CR patients show steepest accumulation — most oligoclonal products")

Finding: In CR patients, the top 10 clonotypes account for 30–45% of all Product reads, indicating highly focused expansion. PD patients show a flatter accumulation curve, consistent with less efficient clonal selection.

Top Clone Fraction by Response

top1 <- top_clones %>%
  filter(sample_type == "Product", rank == 1) %>%
  left_join(patient_meta %>% select(patient_id, clinical_response), by = "patient_id")

ggplot(top1, aes(x = reorder(patient_id, -clone_fraction), y = clone_fraction,
                  fill = clinical_response.x)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.7, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(clone_fraction * 100, 1), "%")),
            vjust = -0.5, color = "#e0eef5", size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(x = "Patient", y = "Top Clone Fraction",
       fill = "Response",
       title = "Dominant Clone Fraction per Patient",
       subtitle = "Fraction of total Product reads held by the single most expanded clone") +
  ylim(0, max(top1$clone_fraction) * 1.2)

Shared Clonotype Analysis

We examine the overlap between Apheresis and Product repertoires to determine what fraction of the Product’s clones were detectable in the starting material.

aph_clones <- tcr_data %>%
  filter(sample_type == "Apheresis") %>%
  select(patient_id, cdr3_aa) %>%
  distinct()

prod_clones <- tcr_data %>%
  filter(sample_type == "Product") %>%
  select(patient_id, cdr3_aa) %>%
  distinct()

overlap_summary <- prod_clones %>%
  group_by(patient_id) %>%
  summarise(
    total_product = n(),
    shared = sum(cdr3_aa %in% aph_clones$cdr3_aa[aph_clones$patient_id == patient_id[1]]),
    pct_shared = round(shared / total_product * 100, 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

overlap_summary %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Apheresis–Product clonotype overlap")
Apheresis–Product clonotype overlap
patient_id total_product shared pct_shared
PT-001 2029 208 10.3
PT-002 940 458 48.7
PT-003 1123 212 18.9
PT-004 2474 250 10.1
PT-005 1913 317 16.6
PT-006 2853 540 18.9 
overlap_summary %>%
  left_join(patient_meta, by = "patient_id") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = patient_id, y = pct_shared, fill = clinical_response)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.7, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(pct_shared, "%")),
            vjust = -0.5, color = "#e0eef5", size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  labs(x = "Patient", y = "% Product Clones Found in Apheresis",
       fill = "Response",
       title = "Shared Clonotype Overlap",
       subtitle = "Majority of Product clones originate from the Apheresis starting material") +
  ylim(0, 100)

Conclusions & Next Steps

Summary of findings:

  1. Manufacturing drives extreme oligoclonal expansion — top 10 Product clones capture 15–45% of reads.
  2. CR patients show the most concentrated Product repertoires, suggesting efficient clonal selection is therapeutically beneficial.
  3. The majority of dominant Product clones are traceable back to the Apheresis starting material.
  4. PD patients exhibit more diffuse Product repertoires with less dominant clonal expansion.

Next steps:

  • Apply edgeR differential abundance framework to formally identify significantly expanding clonotypes (FDR-controlled).
  • Compute AUC-based clonal kinetics if longitudinal timepoints are available.
  • Investigate V gene segment bias in expanded vs. non-expanded clonotypes.
  • Correlate clonal expansion metrics with additional clinical endpoints.
